description: Comprehensive materials knowledge for millwrights — bearings, couplings, mechanical seals, packing, shim stock, grout, alignment tools, vibration equipment, lubricants, fasteners, and the specifications that matter in the field.

bearings.overview: Bearings are the heart of rotating equipment. Wrong bearing selection, installation, or lubrication is the #1 cause of premature equipment failure.

deep_groove.use: General purpose, moderate radial and light axial loads. Most common bearing type.

angular_contact.use: Combined radial and axial loads. Often used in pairs (back-to-back or face-to-face) for bidirectional thrust.
angular_contact.note: Contact angle determines thrust capacity — 15, 25, or 40 degrees.

self_aligning.use: Where shaft deflection or housing misalignment exists. Forgiving of alignment errors.

cylindrical.use: Heavy radial loads, high speed. Allows axial shaft movement (thermal growth). Common in electric motors.

tapered_roller.use: Combined heavy radial and axial loads. Pumps, gearboxes, conveyor idlers. Always used in pairs.
tapered_roller.setting: Requires endplay or preload setting during installation. Too tight = overheating. Too loose = vibration.

spherical_roller.use: Very heavy radial loads with moderate thrust. Self-aligning capability. Vibrating screens, crushers, heavy conveyors.
spherical_roller.note: Most expensive standard bearing type but handles the worst conditions.

ball_thrust.use: Pure axial loads, low to moderate speed.

spherical_roller_thrust.use: Extremely heavy axial loads. Vertical pumps, extruders.

sleeve_bearings.description: Plain journal bearings — shaft rides on oil film, no rolling elements.
sleeve_bearings.use: Large turbines, compressors, generators. Very high speed, heavy loads.
sleeve_bearings.clearance: Typically 0.001 to 0.002 inch per inch of journal diameter. Too tight = wiping. Too loose = vibration.
sleeve_bearings.oil_supply: Ring-oiled (gravity), pressure-fed (oil pump), or oil mist. Critical — loss of oil = instant bearing failure.

bearings.brands: SKF, Timken, FAG (Schaeffler), NSK, NTN, Koyo, Dodge, Rexnord, Cooper
bearings.identification: Bearing number tells you everything: 6310-2RS = 6(deep groove) 3(medium series) 10(50mm bore) 2RS(double sealed). Learn the numbering system.
bearings.storage: Store flat in original packaging. Cool, dry, vibration-free. Do NOT unwrap until ready to install. Contamination is the enemy.
bearings.common_mistake: Driving a bearing onto a shaft by hitting the outer race. Force must be applied to the INNER race only (the race being press-fitted). Hitting outer race damages rolling elements.

flexible_jaw.use: Light duty, pumps under 50 HP. Easy to install and replace spider insert.
flexible_jaw.misalignment_tolerance: Up to 1 degree angular, 0.015 inch parallel

gear_coupling.description: Hardened gear teeth mesh between hub and sleeve. High torque capacity.
gear_coupling.use: High power, high speed — turbines, compressors, large pumps.
gear_coupling.lubrication: Requires grease — gear teeth will wear without lubrication.
gear_coupling.alignment: Tight alignment required despite gear tooth flexibility. Recommended 0.002 inch/inch TIR.

disc_coupling.description: Flexible metallic disc packs transmit torque.
disc_coupling.use: Moderate to high power. No lubrication required. Good for high temperature.
disc_coupling.advantage: Zero backlash, maintenance-free, handles axial movement.

diaphragm_coupling.use: High speed, high power — turbomachinery. Single or multi-diaphragm.
diaphragm_coupling.advantage: Highest speed rating of any coupling type. No lubrication.

grid_coupling.description: Spring steel grid element between hubs absorbs shock.
grid_coupling.use: Crushers, mills, conveyors — applications with shock loading.

spacer_coupling.description: Any coupling type with a spacer element between hubs.
spacer_coupling.use: Allows removal of pump/compressor without moving motor. Standard length per API 610: 7 inches minimum.

couplings.common_mistake: Installing coupling hubs with wrong fit — too tight damages shaft, too loose allows fretting. Check bore/shaft fit tolerance. Typical: 0.0005 to 0.001 inch interference for press fit.

mechanical_seals.overview: Mechanical seals prevent process fluid leakage at the shaft penetration through the pump casing.

single_seal.use: Standard applications. Seal faces run against each other with thin fluid film between them.
single_seal.flush: API Plan 11 (recirculation from discharge), Plan 13 (recirculation from seal chamber), Plan 32 (external flush)

double_seal.use: Hazardous, toxic, or volatile fluids. Two sets of seal faces with barrier fluid between them.
double_seal.barrier: Pressurized barrier fluid (Plan 53A/53B/53C) or unpressurized buffer fluid (Plan 52).
double_seal.note: Double seal provides containment — if primary seal fails, barrier fluid contains leakage.

cartridge_seal.description: Pre-assembled, pre-set seal unit. Slides onto shaft as a complete assembly.
cartridge_seal.advantage: Eliminates field setting of seal face compression. Much faster installation. Fewer errors.
cartridge_seal.note: ALWAYS use cartridge seals when available. Field-set seals have much higher failure rate due to incorrect setting height.

mechanical_seals.brands: John Crane (Type 1, Type 2100, Type 4610), Flowserve (SLC, ISC, GASPAC), EagleBurgmann (M7N, MFL85N), AES (CDSA, BDSA)

face_materials.carbon_vs_silicon_carbide: Carbon (soft face) runs against SiC or tungsten carbide (hard face). Carbon is self-lubricating.
face_materials.SiC_vs_SiC: Both hard faces — for abrasive services. Better wear resistance but requires clean flush.
face_materials.tungsten_carbide: Extremely hard. For abrasive slurries, high-pressure service.

mechanical_seals.common_failure_causes: Dry running (no flush = instant face damage), contamination (particles between faces), thermal shock (rapid temperature change), incorrect setting height (too much spring compression = face overload, too little = leakage)
mechanical_seals.common_mistake: Touching seal faces with bare hands. Skin oil contaminates the lapped faces. Always handle with clean gloves and keep faces wrapped until installation.

epoxy_grout.brands: Masterflow 648 (BASF), Chockfast Orange (ITW), Five Star DP Epoxy, Escoweld 7505 (ITW)
epoxy_grout.use: Equipment baseplates, sole plates, compressor and turbine foundations. THE standard for rotating equipment.
epoxy_grout.advantage: High compressive strength (12000-16000 psi), chemical resistant, vibration dampening, no shrinkage.
epoxy_grout.mixing: Three components — resin, hardener, aggregate. Mix per manufacturer instructions exactly. Temperature sensitive — resin viscosity changes with temperature.
epoxy_grout.pour_temperature: Minimum 40F, maximum 90F for most products. Below 60F, preheat materials. Above 90F, cool materials or pour early morning.
epoxy_grout.pot_life: 30-60 minutes depending on temperature. In hot weather, pot life can be as short as 15 minutes. Mix small batches.
epoxy_grout.cure_time: 24-72 hours to full cure depending on temperature. Do not apply load until fully cured.
epoxy_grout.common_mistake: Not enough aggregate. Epoxy grout without proper aggregate loading generates excessive heat during cure (exothermic reaction) and can crack.

cementitious_grout.use: Non-critical equipment, structural baseplates. Lower cost than epoxy.
cementitious_grout.limitation: Lower strength than epoxy. Can shrink if not properly mixed. Not chemical resistant.
cementitious_grout.mixing: Controlled water ratio is critical. Too much water = shrinkage and low strength. Use manufacturer's recommended water ratio.

grout.surface_preparation: Concrete must be clean, roughened (chipped or sandblasted), saturated surface dry (SSD). Remove all oil, grease, laitance. Forms must be leak-proof — epoxy grout is thin and will find any gap.

dial_indicators.resolution: 0.001 inch (1 mil) standard. 0.0001 inch (0.1 mil) for precision work.
dial_indicators.mounting: Magnetic base or bracket clamped to coupling hub. Must be rigid — flex in bracket gives false readings.

laser_alignment.brands: Fixturlaser (Acoem), SKF TKSA, Hamar, Easy-Laser, Pruftechnik (Fluke)
laser_alignment.advantage: Faster than dial indicators, stores data, calculates shim corrections, thermal growth compensation, live adjustment mode.
laser_alignment.accuracy: 0.0001 inch (0.1 mil) — better than most dial indicator setups.
laser_alignment.practical_note: Laser systems save hours on alignment. Worth the investment for any shop doing regular alignment work. But you must still understand alignment principles — the laser is a tool, not a brain.

straightedge_and_feeler_gauge.use: Rough alignment before precision alignment. Get within 0.010-0.020 inch first, then use dial indicators or laser for final.

thickeners.polyurea: Electric motor bearings — long life, quiet

grease.compatibility: NOT all greases are compatible. Mixing incompatible greases causes softening or hardening. Check manufacturer compatibility chart. When in doubt, purge old grease completely before switching.
grease.common_mistake: Over-greasing bearings. More grease is NOT better. Excess grease causes churning, heat, and premature failure. Follow manufacturer fill quantity — typically 30-50% of bearing free space.

oil.viscosity_grades: ISO VG 32 (light), 46, 68 (medium), 100, 150, 220, 320 (heavy). Higher number = thicker oil.
oil.selection: Based on bearing speed, load, and temperature. Manufacturer specifies grade. Using wrong viscosity is like using wrong blood type.

analysis.particle_count: ISO 4406 cleanliness code. Target depends on equipment — hydraulics need cleaner oil than gearboxes.
analysis.wear_metals: Iron, copper, lead, tin indicate which component is wearing.
analysis.water: Above 200 ppm = investigate source. Above 500 ppm = change oil immediately.
analysis.viscosity: Change greater than 10% from new oil = oxidation or contamination.

SAE_grades.grade_2: Low carbon. General purpose, non-critical. No head markings.
SAE_grades.grade_5: Medium carbon, quenched and tempered. 120,000 psi tensile. Three radial lines on head. Most common structural bolt.
SAE_grades.grade_8: Alloy steel, quenched and tempered. 150,000 psi tensile. Six radial lines on head. High-strength critical applications.

metric_classes.class_8_8: Equivalent to SAE Grade 5. Marked 8.8 on head.
metric_classes.class_12_9: Highest standard metric grade. Marked 12.9. Equivalent to above Grade 8.

stainless_steel.A2_70: 304 stainless, 70 ksi tensile. For corrosive environments.
stainless_steel.A4_80: 316 stainless, 80 ksi tensile. Marine and chemical environments.
stainless_steel.CAUTION: Stainless bolts gall easily — ALWAYS use anti-seize compound on stainless fasteners. Without anti-seize, they seize during tightening and cannot be removed.

fasteners.common_mistake: Using Grade 2 hardware where Grade 5 is specified. It looks the same but is only half the strength. Always verify grade markings on head.
